# COMP 110 Data Analysis Project 
*By Reese Bunner and Mary Christian*

## My Idea
The course should slow down during more difficult topics because students may struggle to keep up with the pace.

## Analysis
I analyzed survey data from COMP110 students to understand how they feel about the pace of the course and how it may impact their understanding. I used Python functions to count how many students selected each rating and created visualizations to better interpret the data.

## Visualization 1

<img src="/comp110-project/static/imgs/pace.png" width="500">

*Figure 1: Distribution of student ratings for course pace.*

Most students selected values around 4 and 5, suggesting the pace is generally moderate but slightly fast. There are also several higher ratings (6 and 7), indicating that some students feel the course moves too quickly. Very few students selected low values, meaning almost no students think the course is too slow.

## Visualization 2

<img src="/comp110-project/static/imgs/understanding.png" width="500">

*Figure 2: Distribution of student understanding ratings.*

This graph shows how students rate their understanding of the course material. Most responses fall in the middle range, suggesting that many students feel they understand the material but not completely. There are also some lower ratings, indicating that a portion of students feel lost at times. This supports the idea that if the course moves too quickly during difficult topics, it may negatively impact student understanding.

## Visualization 3

<img src="/comp110-project/static/imgs/year.png" width="500">

*Figure 3: Distribution of students by year.*

This graph shows the distribution of students by year. Most responses come from the classes of ’28 and ’27, with fewer from ’26 and ’29. This indicates the survey sample is skewed toward underclassmen, which may limit how well the results represent upperclassmen.


## Additional Insight
Most responses came from underclassmen (Class of ’27 and ’28), which suggests the results may reflect newer students more than upperclassmen. This could slightly limit how representative the findings are for all students.

## Conclusion
Based on this analysis, most students perceive the course pace as moderate but slightly fast, and some report lower levels of understanding. This suggests that slowing down during more difficult topics could improve comprehension and reduce confusion.

A potential trade-off is that less content may be covered during the semester, but this change would likely improve overall learning outcomes and help more students stay on track. Additionally, because the data is somewhat skewed toward underclassmen, future analysis could explore whether upperclassmen have different experiences with course pace.
